topic: A person discovers their teenage diary has been published online
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara found the laptop open on her desk, a screen glowing with an unfamiliar URL. Her breath hitched in her throat as she clicked. There, under a title that made her stomach clench, was her diary. Every embarrassing confession, every secret crush, every raw outpouring of her seventeen years was plastered across a public forum. Her fingers trembled as they hovered over the screen, then flew to the refresh button, desperately searching for a glitch, a mistake. When the familiar, messy script filled the screen, a cold knot tightened in her chest. She stood up abruptly, knocking her chair backward with a loud scrape. Her vision blurred for a moment, and she backed away from the monitor, hands flying up to cover her mouth, a low, ragged sound escaping her lips.

[story 2]

Mark was scrolling through his phone, intending only to check social media, when a notification popped up. It was a link to a blog post. Curiosity, a dangerous itch, compelled him to tap. What he saw made the blood drain from his face. It was his private journal, the one he’d hidden under his mattress, now live on the internet, complete with cryptic descriptions of his anxieties. He snatched the phone away, his heart hammering against his ribs like a trapped bird. He paced the small room, his movements jerky and uncontrolled. He kept glancing over his shoulder, the silence of the apartment suddenly amplifying the frantic drumming inside his ears.

[story 3]

The envelope felt wrong in Sarah’s hands—too crisp, too official. Inside, nestled on the parchment paper, was a printout. Her teenage diary, the one she’d thought was safely locked away in the attic, was reproduced, digitized, and shared on a popular gossip site. Sarah stared at the page, her knuckles turning white where she gripped the paper. A wave of nausea washed over her, making her grip the edges of the desk until her nails dug into her palms. She let out a shaky, almost silent gasp, her eyes wide and unfocused, as if trying to process a reality that had just fractured.
